verification_note: v3.0: Every BNS/BNSS/BSA fact in this dataset was directly verified against the official government PDFs (legislative.gov.in, mha.gov.in, indiacode.nic.in), correcting several section-number errors from v2.0.
description: Golden evaluation dataset covering the full legal corpus: Constitution of India, Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023 (BNS), Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita 2023 (BNSS), and Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023 (BSA).

categories.A: Answerable from the corpus - system must answer with citations
categories.B: Out of scope - system must refuse

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 21 say about the right to life and personal liberty?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 21 states that no person shall be deprived of his life or personal liberty except according to procedure established by law.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 14 of the Constitution guarantee?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 14 guarantees that the State shall not deny to any person equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws within the territory of India.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 19 of the Constitution guarantee?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 19 guarantees to all citizens freedom of speech and expression, the right to assemble peaceably and without arms, the right to form associations or unions, the right to move freely throughout India, the right to reside and settle in any part of India, and the right to practise any profession or carry on any occupation, trade or business.

qa_pairs.question: What remedy does Article 32 provide for enforcement of fundamental rights?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 32 guarantees the right to move the Supreme Court by appropriate proceedings for enforcement of fundamental rights. The Supreme Court has power to issue directions, orders or writs including habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto and certiorari.

qa_pairs.question: What is the procedure for amendment of the Constitution under Article 368?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Article 368, an amendment may be initiated by a Bill in either House of Parliament. The Bill must be passed in each House by a majority of the total membership and by not less than two-thirds of members present and voting. The Bill is then presented to the President who gives assent. For amendments to certain specified provisions, ratification by Legislatures of not less than one-half of the States is also required.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Preamble of the Constitution of India declare?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: The Preamble declares India to be a Sovereign Socialist Secular Democratic Republic. It resolves to secure to all citizens justice, liberty, equality, and to promote fraternity assuring the dignity of the individual and the unity and integrity of the Nation.

qa_pairs.question: What are the qualifications required to be elected as President of India?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Article 58, a person must be a citizen of India, have completed thirty-five years of age, and be qualified for election as a member of the House of the People. A person holding any office of profit under any Government is not eligible.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 356 provide regarding President's Rule in a State?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 356 provides that if the President is satisfied that the government of a State cannot be carried on in accordance with the Constitution, the President may assume to himself all or any of the functions of the Government of the State and powers vested in the Governor.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 44 provide regarding a Uniform Civil Code?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 44 provides that the State shall endeavour to secure for the citizens a uniform civil code throughout the territory of India. This is a Directive Principle of State Policy in Part IV of the Constitution.

qa_pairs.question: What are the Fundamental Duties listed under Article 51A?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 51A lists duties of every citizen including abiding by the Constitution, cherishing ideals of the national struggle, upholding sovereignty and integrity of India, defending the country, promoting harmony and common brotherhood, protecting the natural environment, developing scientific temper, safeguarding public property, and striving towards excellence.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 22 protect citizens against regarding arrest and detention?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 22 provides that no person who is arrested shall be detained without being informed of the grounds of arrest. Every arrested person has the right to consult a legal practitioner of their choice and shall be produced before the nearest Magistrate within twenty-four hours.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 352 deal with regarding National Emergency?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 352 provides that if the President is satisfied that a grave emergency exists whereby the security of India or any part is threatened by war, external aggression or armed rebellion, he may by Proclamation declare a National Emergency. Such a proclamation must be approved by both Houses of Parliament.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 13 provide regarding laws inconsistent with fundamental rights?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 13 provides that all laws in force before commencement of the Constitution that are inconsistent with Part III shall be void to the extent of such inconsistency. The State shall not make any law which takes away or abridges the rights conferred by Part III and any such law shall be void.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 17 of the Constitution deal with?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 17 abolishes untouchability and forbids its practice in any form. The enforcement of any disability arising out of untouchability shall be an offence punishable in accordance with law.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 21A provide regarding the right to education?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 21A provides that the State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years in such manner as the State may by law determine.

qa_pairs.question: What is the composition of the Rajya Sabha?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Article 80, the Rajya Sabha shall consist of not more than two hundred and thirty-eight representatives of States and Union territories, and twelve members nominated by the President having special knowledge or practical experience in literature, science, art and social service.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 124 provide regarding the establishment of the Supreme Court?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 124 provides for the establishment of the Supreme Court of India consisting of a Chief Justice of India and such number of other Judges as Parliament may prescribe. Every Judge shall be appointed by the President after consultation with such Judges of the Supreme Court and High Courts as the President deems necessary.

qa_pairs.question: What powers does Article 226 confer on High Courts?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 226 empowers every High Court to issue to any person or authority, including the Government, directions, orders or writs including habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, quo warranto and certiorari for the enforcement of fundamental rights and for any other purpose.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 300A provide regarding the right to property?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 300A provides that no person shall be deprived of his property save by authority of law.

qa_pairs.question: What does Article 12 define as the State for purposes of fundamental rights?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Article 12 defines the State as including the Government and Parliament of India, the Government and Legislature of each State, and all local or other authorities within the territory of India or under the control of the Government of India.

qa_pairs.question: What are the types of punishments to which offenders are liable under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 4 of the BNS, the punishments to which offenders are liable are death, imprisonment for life, imprisonment (rigorous or simple), forfeiture of property, fine, and community service.

qa_pairs.question: What is the definition of culpable homicide under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 100 of the BNS, whoever causes death by doing an act with the intention of causing death, or with the intention of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, or with the knowledge that he is likely by such act to cause death, commits the offence of culpable homicide.

qa_pairs.question: What is the punishment for murder under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 103 of the BNS, whoever commits murder shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine. Murder itself is defined in Section 101 as culpable homicide committed with the intention of causing death or with knowledge that the act is likely to cause death.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita say about general exceptions to criminal liability?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Chapter III of the BNS provides general exceptions including nothing being an offence if done by a child under seven years of age, by a child between seven and twelve who lacks maturity of understanding, by a person of unsound mind incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or by a person incapable of knowing the nature of the act due to intoxication administered without his knowledge or consent.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita say about the right of private defence of the body?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under the BNS, every person has a right, subject to restrictions, to defend his own body and the body of any other person against any offence affecting the human body. This right extends to voluntarily causing death to the assailant only in specific circumstances such as an assault causing reasonable apprehension of death or grievous hurt, or an assault with intention to commit rape, kidnap, or wrongfully confine. The right of private defence in no case extends to inflicting more harm than necessary for the purpose of defence.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita say about the right of private defence of property?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under the BNS, the right of private defence of property extends to voluntarily causing death to a wrong-doer if the offence is robbery, house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, mischief by fire on a building used as a human dwelling, or theft/mischief/house-trespass under circumstances causing reasonable apprehension of death or grievous hurt.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita say about criminal conspiracy?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 61 of the BNS, when two or more persons agree to do or cause to be done an illegal act, or a legal act by illegal means, such an agreement is designated a criminal conspiracy. A person party to a criminal conspiracy to commit an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment for two years or more shall be punished in the same manner as if he had abetted such offence.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita say about abetment of an offence?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Sections 45 and 46 of the BNS, a person abets the doing of a thing who instigates any person to do it, engages with others in a conspiracy for the doing of it, or intentionally aids by any act or illegal omission its doing. Whoever abets an offence shall, if the act abetted is committed in consequence, be punished with the punishment provided for that offence, unless the BNS makes express provision otherwise.

qa_pairs.question: What is the punishment for attempting to commit an offence under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 62 of the BNS, whoever attempts to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment, and does any act towards the commission of the offence, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to one-half of the imprisonment for life or one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for that offence, or with fine, or with both, unless the BNS makes express provision for that attempt.

qa_pairs.question: How does the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita define rape and its punishment?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 63 of the BNS, a man commits rape if he penetrates any part of a woman's body without her consent, or under circumstances such as consent obtained by fear or misconception, or with a woman under eighteen years of age regardless of consent. Under Section 64, rape is punished with rigorous imprisonment of not less than ten years, which may extend to imprisonment for life, along with fine.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita say about dowry death?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 80 of the BNS, where the death of a woman is caused by burns, bodily injury, or otherwise than under normal circumstances within seven years of marriage, and it is shown that she was subjected to cruelty or harassment by her husband or his relatives in connection with a dowry demand shortly before her death, such death is called dowry death and the husband or relative is deemed to have caused it. The punishment is imprisonment for a term of not less than seven years, which may extend to imprisonment for life.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita say about causing miscarriage without a woman's consent?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 88 of the BNS, whoever voluntarily causes a woman with child to miscarry, other than in good faith to save her life, is punishable with imprisonment. Under Section 89, if the miscarriage is caused without the woman's consent, the offender shall be punished with imprisonment for life or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.

qa_pairs.question: How does the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita distinguish culpable homicide from murder?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 100, causing death with the intention of causing death, intending bodily injury likely to cause death, or with knowledge the act is likely to cause death, is culpable homicide. Under Section 101, culpable homicide amounts to murder if the act causing death was done with the intention of causing death, or with intention of causing bodily injury the offender knows is likely to cause death.

qa_pairs.question: What acts does the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita exempt from criminal liability by reason of consent?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Sections 25-30 of the BNS, harm caused with a person's consent is generally not an offence, except intentional causing of death or grievous hurt, unless the harm is not intended and not known to be likely to cause death or grievous hurt, or is done in good faith for the person's benefit. Consent given under fear, misconception, unsoundness of mind, intoxication, or by a person under twelve years is not valid consent under the Sanhita.

qa_pairs.question: What is enhanced punishment under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita for repeat offenders?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 13 of the BNS, whoever, having been convicted of an offence under Chapter X or Chapter XVII with imprisonment of three years or more, commits a like offence again shall be subject to imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years.

qa_pairs.question: Under what circumstances can a police officer arrest a person without a warrant under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 35 of the BNSS, a police officer may arrest without a warrant any person who commits a cognizable offence in his presence, against whom a reasonable complaint or credible information exists of a cognizable offence, who has been proclaimed as an offender, who is found in possession of suspected stolen property, who obstructs a police officer or escapes from lawful custody, or for whom a written requisition for arrest has been received from another officer.

qa_pairs.question: What are the rights of an arrested person under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under the BNSS, an arrested person has the right to be informed of the full particulars of the offence for which he is arrested (Section 47), the right to be informed of bail entitlement if arrested for a non-bailable offence (Section 47), the right to have a relative or friend informed of the arrest (Section 48), and the right to meet an advocate of his choice during interrogation, though not throughout (Section 38).

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita say about the right to meet an advocate during interrogation?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 38 of the BNSS, when any person is arrested and interrogated by the police, he shall be entitled to meet an advocate of his choice during interrogation, though not throughout interrogation.

qa_pairs.question: What is the maximum period for which an arrested person can be detained without being produced before a Magistrate under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 58 of the BNSS, no police officer shall detain a person arrested without warrant in custody for a period longer than is reasonable, and in the absence of a special order of a Magistrate, this period shall not exceed twenty-four hours, exclusive of the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Magistrate's Court.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita say about medical examination of an arrested person?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 53 of the BNSS, when a person is arrested, he shall be examined by a medical officer in the service of the Central or State Government soon after arrest, or by a registered medical practitioner if a medical officer is not available. Where the arrested person is female, the examination shall be made only by or under the supervision of a female medical officer or female registered medical practitioner.

qa_pairs.question: What duty does the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita impose regarding the health and safety of an arrested person?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 56 of the BNSS, it shall be the duty of the person having custody of an accused to take reasonable care of the health and safety of the accused.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita say about searching an arrested person and seized articles?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 49 of the BNSS, when an arrested person cannot be admitted to bail, the arresting officer may search that person and place any seized articles, other than necessary wearing apparel, in safe custody, and a receipt showing the articles taken shall be given to the person. When a female is to be searched, the search shall be made by another female with strict regard to decency.

qa_pairs.question: What special protections does the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita provide for the arrest of women?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 43 of the BNSS, save in exceptional circumstances, no woman shall be arrested after sunset and before sunrise, and where such circumstances exist the woman police officer must obtain prior written permission from a Magistrate of the first class. Additionally, unless circumstances indicate otherwise, a woman's submission to custody on an oral intimation of arrest shall be presumed, and a male police officer shall not touch her person to make the arrest.

qa_pairs.question: What is the procedure under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita for a person who has absconded to avoid a warrant?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 84 of the BNSS, if a Court has reason to believe that a person against whom a warrant has been issued has absconded or is concealing himself, the Court may publish a written proclamation requiring him to appear at a specified place and time not less than thirty days from publication. If the offence is punishable with ten years or more, life imprisonment, or death, and the person fails to appear, the Court may declare him a proclaimed offender.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita say about attachment of property of an absconding person?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 85 of the BNSS, the Court issuing a proclamation against an absconding person may order the attachment of any movable or immovable property belonging to that person. Movable property may be attached by seizure, appointment of a receiver, or an order prohibiting delivery of the property; immovable property, other than revenue-paying land, may be attached by taking possession or appointment of a receiver.

qa_pairs.question: What classes of Criminal Courts does the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita establish?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 6 of the BNSS, besides the High Courts, every State shall have Courts of Session, Judicial Magistrates of the first class, Judicial Magistrates of the second class, and Executive Magistrates.

qa_pairs.question: What are the eligibility requirements for appointment as a Public Prosecutor under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 18 of the BNSS, a person is eligible for appointment as a Public Prosecutor or Additional Public Prosecutor only if he has been in practice as an advocate for not less than seven years. For appointment as a Special Public Prosecutor, a person must have been in practice as an advocate for not less than ten years.

qa_pairs.question: What sentences can different classes of Magistrates pass under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 23 of the BNSS, a Chief Judicial Magistrate may pass any sentence except death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment exceeding seven years. A Magistrate of the first class may pass imprisonment up to three years, a fine up to fifty thousand rupees, or community service. A Magistrate of the second class may pass imprisonment up to one year, a fine up to ten thousand rupees, or community service.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita provide regarding identification of an arrested person?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 54 of the BNSS, where identification of an arrested person by others is necessary for investigation, the Court may, on request of the officer in charge of a police station, direct the arrested person to subject himself to identification. If the person identifying is mentally or physically disabled, the identification process takes place under a Magistrate's supervision using methods the person is comfortable with, and shall be recorded by audio-video electronic means.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita define as a bailable offence and a cognizable offence?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 2 of the BNSS, a bailable offence is one shown as bailable in the First Schedule or made bailable by any other law, and a non-bailable offence is any other offence. A cognizable offence is one for which a police officer may, per the First Schedule or other law, arrest without a warrant.

qa_pairs.question: How does the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam define an admission?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 15 of the BSA, an admission is a statement, oral or documentary or contained in electronic form, which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact, made by specified persons under specified circumstances.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam say about the burden of proof?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 104 of the BSA, whoever desires any Court to give judgment as to any legal right or liability dependent on the existence of facts which he asserts must prove that those facts exist. Under Section 105, the burden of proof lies on the person who would fail if no evidence were given on either side.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam say about confessions made to police officers?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 23 of the BSA, no confession made to a police officer shall be proved against a person accused of any offence. No confession made while in police custody shall be proved unless made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam say about statements made as to the cause of death (dying declarations)?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 26(a) of the BSA, statements made by a person as to the cause of his death, or as to circumstances of the transaction resulting in his death, are relevant facts in cases where the cause of that person's death is in question, whether or not the person was under expectation of death when the statement was made.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam say about expert opinion?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 39 of the BSA, when the Court has to form an opinion on a point of foreign law, science, art, or identity of handwriting or finger impressions, the opinions of persons specially skilled in those areas are relevant facts, and such persons are called experts.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam say about secondary evidence?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 58 of the BSA, secondary evidence includes certified copies, copies made by mechanical processes that ensure accuracy, copies made from or compared with the original, counterparts of documents, oral accounts of a document's contents given by someone who has seen it, and oral or written admissions.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam say about facts that are the occasion, cause, or effect of a fact in issue?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 5 of the BSA, facts which are the occasion, cause, or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts or facts in issue, or which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or afforded an opportunity for their occurrence, are relevant.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam say about the admissibility of electronic records?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 63 of the BSA, information contained in an electronic record produced by a computer, if the conditions specified in that section are satisfied, is deemed to be a document and is admissible as evidence without further proof or production of the original.

qa_pairs.question: Under the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, who bears the burden of proving that an accused's case falls within a general exception?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 108 of the BSA, when a person is accused of an offence, the burden of proving circumstances bringing the case within any General Exception in the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, or any special exception or proviso, lies upon the accused, and the Court shall presume the absence of such circumstances.

qa_pairs.question: What presumption does the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam make regarding secure electronic records and signatures?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 86 of the BSA, in proceedings involving a secure electronic record, the Court shall presume, unless the contrary is proved, that the record has not been altered since the point in time to which its secure status relates. The Court shall also presume a secure electronic signature was affixed by the subscriber with the intention of signing or approving the record.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Code on Wages, 2019 include and exclude from the definition of 'wages'?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 2(y) of the Code on Wages, wages means all remuneration expressed in money terms including basic pay, dearness allowance, and retaining allowance. It excludes bonus, house-accommodation value, employer PF/pension contributions, conveyance allowance, HRA, overtime allowance, commission, and gratuity — but if these exclusions collectively exceed 50% of total remuneration, the excess is added back into wages.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Code on Wages, 2019 say about the floor wage?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 9, the Central Government shall fix a floor wage taking into account minimum living standards of a worker, and different floor wages may be fixed for different geographical areas. The minimum rates of wages fixed by any State cannot be less than the floor wage, and if a State's existing minimum wage is already higher than the floor wage, the State cannot reduce it.

qa_pairs.question: What time limits does the Code on Wages, 2019 set for payment of wages?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 17, wages must be paid before the end of the shift for daily-wage workers, before the weekly holiday for weekly-wage workers, within two days of the fortnight's end for fortnightly workers, and before the 7th day of the following month for monthly-wage workers. If a worker is removed, dismissed, or retrenched, wages must be paid within two working days.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Code on Wages, 2019 say about minimum and maximum bonus eligibility?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 26, every eligible employee who has worked at least 30 days in an accounting year must be paid an annual minimum bonus of 8.33% of wages earned or one hundred rupees, whichever is higher, regardless of whether the employer has allocable surplus. Where allocable surplus exceeds the minimum bonus, the employer must pay a proportionate bonus subject to a maximum of 20% of wages earned.

qa_pairs.question: What penalties does the Code on Wages, 2019 prescribe for underpayment of wages?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 54, an employer who pays an employee less than the amount due is punishable with a fine up to fifty thousand rupees. If convicted again for a similar offence within five years, the employer is punishable with imprisonment up to three months or a fine up to one lakh rupees, or both.

qa_pairs.question: What is a Works Committee under the Industrial Relations Code, 2020 and when must one be constituted?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 3, an industrial establishment with 100 or more workers may be required by the appropriate Government to constitute a Works Committee with representatives of employer and workers, where worker representatives shall not be fewer than employer representatives. Its duty is to promote amity and good relations between employer and workers and address matters of common interest.

qa_pairs.question: What is a Grievance Redressal Committee under the Industrial Relations Code, 2020?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 4, every industrial establishment employing 20 or more workers must have one or more Grievance Redressal Committees, with equal representation of employer and workers, a maximum of 10 members, and adequate representation of women workers. The Committee must resolve individual grievance applications within 30 days, with an appeal to conciliation available within 60 days if unresolved.

qa_pairs.question: What are the requirements for registering a Trade Union under the Industrial Relations Code, 2020?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 6, any seven or more members of a Trade Union may apply for registration. No Trade Union of workers shall be registered unless at least 10% of the workers, or 100 workers, whichever is less, engaged in the establishment are members on the date of application, and a registered union must continue to maintain this membership threshold, subject to a minimum of seven members.

qa_pairs.question: To which industrial establishments does the requirement for Standing Orders apply under the Industrial Relations Code, 2020?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 28, the Standing Orders chapter applies to every industrial establishment where 300 or more workers are employed or were employed on any day of the preceding twelve months.

qa_pairs.question: What notice periods does the Industrial Relations Code, 2020 require before a strike or lock-out?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 62, no worker may go on strike without giving 60 days' notice, and the strike cannot begin within 14 days of that notice or before the date specified in the notice. The same notice periods apply to lock-outs by employers. Strikes and lock-outs are also prohibited during pendency of conciliation, arbitration, or Tribunal proceedings and for a period afterward.

qa_pairs.question: What are the general duties of an employer under the Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code, 2020?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 6, every employer must ensure the workplace is free from hazards likely to cause injury or disease, comply with occupational safety and health standards, provide free annual health examinations to prescribed classes of employees, maintain a safe working environment, ensure proper disposal of hazardous waste, and issue appointment letters to every employee.

qa_pairs.question: When must an establishment appoint safety officers under the Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code, 2020?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 22, an employer must appoint safety officers in a factory with 500 or more workers, a factory carrying on a hazardous process with 250 or more workers, building or construction work with 250 or more workers, or a mine with 100 or more workers ordinarily employed.

qa_pairs.question: What do the daily and weekly working hour limits under the Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code, 2020 provide?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 25, no worker shall be required or allowed to work more than eight hours in a day. Under Section 26, no worker shall be allowed to work in an establishment for more than six days in any one week, subject to certain exemptions for motor transport undertakings.

qa_pairs.question: What annual leave with wages is a worker entitled to under the Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code, 2020?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 32, a worker who has worked 180 days or more in a calendar year is entitled to one day of leave for every 20 days worked, or for every 15 days worked in the case of an adolescent worker or a worker employed below ground in a mine. Unused leave may be carried forward up to a maximum of 30 days, and workers may encash leave exceeding that limit.

qa_pairs.question: What contribution rates apply to the Employees' Provident Fund under the Code on Social Security, 2020?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 16, the employer's contribution to the Provident Fund is 10% of wages for each employee, matched by an equal employee contribution, though this is raised to 12% for establishments specified by Central Government notification. An employee may voluntarily contribute more, but the employer is not obligated to match any amount above its required contribution.

qa_pairs.question: What makes an agreement a valid contract under the Indian Contract Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 10, all agreements are contracts if made by the free consent of parties competent to contract, for a lawful consideration and with a lawful object, and are not expressly declared void by the Act.

qa_pairs.question: What constitutes coercion and undue influence under the Indian Contract Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 15, coercion is committing or threatening to commit any act forbidden by law, or unlawfully detaining or threatening to detain property, to force a person into an agreement. Under Section 16, undue influence occurs where one party is in a position to dominate the will of the other, such as through a fiduciary relationship, and uses that position to obtain an unfair advantage.

qa_pairs.question: Can an agreement without consideration be a valid contract under the Indian Contract Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 25, an agreement without consideration is generally void, except in three cases: it is in writing and registered and made out of natural love and affection between parties in a near relation; it is a promise to compensate someone who has already voluntarily done something for the promisor; or it is a written, signed promise to pay a debt that is time-barred under the limitation law.

qa_pairs.question: How does the Indian Contract Act define a contract of indemnity and a contract of guarantee?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 124, a contract of indemnity is one where a party promises to save the other from loss caused by the promisor's own conduct or the conduct of any other person. Under Section 126, a contract of guarantee is a contract to perform the promise or discharge the liability of a third person in case of their default; the person giving the guarantee is the surety, the person in default is the principal debtor, and the person to whom the guarantee is given is the creditor.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Code of Civil Procedure say about the principle of res judicata?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 11, no court shall try any suit or issue in which the matter directly and substantially in issue has already been directly and substantially in issue in a former suit between the same parties, litigating under the same title, in a court competent to try the subsequent suit, and which has been heard and finally decided by that court.

qa_pairs.question: Where must a suit relating to immovable property be instituted under the Code of Civil Procedure?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 16, suits for recovery of immovable property, partition of immovable property, foreclosure, sale or redemption of a mortgage, or determination of a right or interest in immovable property must be instituted in the court within whose local limits the property is situated.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Code of Civil Procedure say about a court's power to award interest on a money decree?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 34, where a decree is for payment of money, the court may order interest at a rate it deems reasonable on the principal sum from the date of the suit to the date of the decree, and further interest not exceeding six percent per annum from the date of the decree to the date of payment, except that for commercial transactions the further interest rate may exceed six percent up to the contractual rate.

qa_pairs.question: How does the Transfer of Property Act define a sale of immovable property?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 54, a sale is a transfer of ownership in exchange for a price paid, promised, or part-paid and part-promised. For tangible immovable property valued at one hundred rupees or more, the transfer can only be made by a registered instrument; for property valued below that, it may be made by registered instrument or by delivery of the property.

qa_pairs.question: What types of mortgages does the Transfer of Property Act recognise?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 58, the Transfer of Property Act recognises a simple mortgage, mortgage by conditional sale, usufructuary mortgage, English mortgage, mortgage by deposit of title-deeds, and anomalous mortgage, which is any mortgage not falling into the other five categories.

qa_pairs.question: What is a mortgagor's right to redeem under the Transfer of Property Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 60, at any time after the principal money has become due, the mortgagor has a right, on payment or tender of the mortgage money, to require the mortgagee to deliver the mortgage deed and related documents, deliver possession of the property if the mortgagee holds it, and re-transfer the property or execute an acknowledgment that the mortgagee's interest has been extinguished.

qa_pairs.question: Which contracts cannot be specifically enforced under the Specific Relief Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 14, a contract cannot be specifically enforced where a party has already obtained substituted performance under Section 20, where performance involves a continuous duty the court cannot supervise, where the contract is so dependent on personal qualifications of the parties that the court cannot enforce its material terms, or where the contract is in its nature determinable.

qa_pairs.question: When may a perpetual injunction be granted under the Specific Relief Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 38, a perpetual injunction may be granted to prevent breach of an obligation existing in the plaintiff's favour. Where the defendant invades or threatens to invade the plaintiff's property rights, the court may grant it where the defendant is a trustee for the plaintiff, where there is no standard to ascertain actual damage, where compensation in money would not afford adequate relief, or where it is necessary to prevent multiplicity of judicial proceedings.

qa_pairs.question: What happens if a suit, appeal, or application is filed after the period of limitation prescribed by the Limitation Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 3, every suit instituted, appeal preferred, or application made after the prescribed period of limitation shall be dismissed, even if the opposing party has not raised limitation as a defence, subject to the extensions and exclusions provided elsewhere in the Act.

qa_pairs.question: What is the limitation period for a suit for specific performance of a contract or for compensation for breach of contract under the Limitation Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under the Schedule to the Limitation Act, a suit for specific performance of a contract must be filed within three years from the date fixed for performance, or if no date is fixed, from when the plaintiff has notice that performance is refused. A suit for compensation for breach of contract not otherwise specifically provided for must also be filed within three years of the breach.
qa_pairs.source_hint: Schedule, Part II, Articles 54-55, Limitation Act 1963

qa_pairs.question: What does the General Clauses Act say about the effect of repealing an enactment?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 6, unless a different intention appears, repealing an enactment does not revive anything not in force at the time of repeal, does not affect the previous operation of the repealed enactment or anything already done under it, does not affect rights, privileges, obligations or liabilities already acquired or incurred, does not affect penalties already incurred for offences under the repealed enactment, and does not affect any pending investigation, legal proceeding or remedy in respect of such rights or liabilities, which may continue as if the repealing Act had not been passed.

qa_pairs.question: What general rule does the General Clauses Act establish regarding gender and number in legislative drafting?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 13, unless the subject or context is repugnant, words importing the masculine gender in Central Acts and Regulations are taken to include females, and words in the singular include the plural, and vice versa.

qa_pairs.question: What are the minimum age requirements for driving different classes of motor vehicles under the Motor Vehicles Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 4, no person under 18 years may drive a motor vehicle in a public place, except that a motorcycle with engine capacity not exceeding 50cc may be driven from age 16. No person under 20 years may drive a transport vehicle.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Motor Vehicles Act require regarding possession of a driving licence?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 3, no person shall drive a motor vehicle in any public place unless they hold an effective driving licence authorising them to drive that vehicle, and no person may drive a transport vehicle unless their licence specifically entitles them to do so.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Motor Vehicles Act say about compulsory insurance for motor vehicles?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Chapter XI of the Motor Vehicles Act, no person shall use a motor vehicle in a public place, or allow it to be used, unless there is a valid third-party insurance policy in force covering the vehicle, as required under Section 146.

qa_pairs.question: How does the Negotiable Instruments Act define a cheque?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 6, a cheque is a bill of exchange drawn on a specified banker and not expressed to be payable otherwise than on demand, and includes the electronic image of a truncated cheque and a cheque in electronic form.

qa_pairs.question: What is the punishment for dishonour of a cheque due to insufficient funds under the Negotiable Instruments Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 138, a person whose cheque is dishonoured due to insufficient funds commits an offence punishable with imprisonment up to two years, or a fine up to twice the cheque amount, or both, provided the cheque was presented within six months, the payee issued a demand notice within thirty days of learning of the dishonour, and the drawer failed to pay within fifteen days of receiving that notice.

qa_pairs.question: What presumptions does the Negotiable Instruments Act make in favour of the holder of a negotiable instrument?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 118, until the contrary is proved, every negotiable instrument is presumed to have been made for consideration, to bear its true date, and to have been accepted, negotiated, and transferred within a reasonable time and before maturity. Under Section 139, in cheque dishonour cases it is presumed that the cheque was received for discharge of a debt or liability, unless the contrary is proved.

qa_pairs.question: What legal recognition does the Information Technology Act give to electronic records and electronic signatures?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 4, where any law requires information to be in writing or printed form, that requirement is deemed satisfied if the information is rendered in electronic form and remains accessible for subsequent reference. Under Section 5, a similar deeming provision extends to authentication requirements satisfied through electronic signature.

qa_pairs.question: What does the Information Technology Act say about punishment for identity theft and cheating by personation using a computer resource?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 66C, whoever fraudulently or dishonestly makes use of the electronic signature, password, or unique identification feature of another person is punishable with imprisonment up to three years and a fine up to one lakh rupees. Under Section 66D, cheating by personation using a computer resource or communication device carries the same punishment.

qa_pairs.question: Under what conditions is an intermediary exempt from liability for third-party content under the Information Technology Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 79, an intermediary is not liable for third-party information hosted or made available by it, provided its function is limited to providing access and it does not initiate the transmission, select the receiver, or modify the content, and it observes due diligence and promptly removes or disables access to unlawful content upon acquiring actual knowledge or government notification.

qa_pairs.question: What are the pecuniary jurisdiction limits of the District, State, and National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commissions under the Consumer Protection Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Sections 34, 47, and 58, the District Commission has jurisdiction over complaints where consideration does not exceed one crore rupees, the State Commission over complaints exceeding one crore but not exceeding ten crore rupees, and the National Commission over complaints exceeding ten crore rupees.

qa_pairs.question: When is a product manufacturer liable in a product liability action under the Consumer Protection Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 84, a product manufacturer is liable if the product contains a manufacturing defect, is defective in design, deviates from manufacturing specifications, does not conform to an express warranty, or lacks adequate instructions or warnings about proper usage. The manufacturer is liable even if it proves it was not negligent or fraudulent in making the express warranty.

qa_pairs.question: What powers does the Central Consumer Protection Authority have to act against false or misleading advertisements?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 21, where the Central Authority finds an advertisement false or misleading, it may direct the trader, manufacturer, endorser, advertiser or publisher to discontinue or modify it, impose a penalty up to ten lakh rupees on the manufacturer or endorser (up to fifty lakh rupees for repeat contraventions), and prohibit an endorser from making endorsements for up to one year, or three years for repeat contraventions.

qa_pairs.question: What is the time limit for a public authority to respond to a request under the Right to Information Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 7, a Public Information Officer must respond within thirty days of receiving a request, either providing the information or rejecting it with reasons. Where the information concerns the life or liberty of a person, it must be provided within forty-eight hours.

qa_pairs.question: What categories of information are exempt from disclosure under the Right to Information Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 8, exemptions include information prejudicial to the sovereignty and security of India, information forbidden by a court, breach of parliamentary privilege, trade secrets and commercial confidence, information held in a fiduciary relationship, information that would endanger life or safety, information impeding investigation or prosecution, cabinet papers, and personal information.

qa_pairs.question: What penalties can be imposed on a Public Information Officer for non-compliance under the Right to Information Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 20, the Information Commission may impose a penalty of two hundred and fifty rupees per day of delay, up to a maximum of twenty-five thousand rupees, on a Public Information Officer who without reasonable cause refuses a request, fails to furnish information in time, malafidely denies a request, or knowingly gives incorrect or misleading information, after giving the officer an opportunity to be heard.

qa_pairs.question: What rights does a Data Principal have under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Sections 11 to 14, a Data Principal has the right to obtain a summary of personal data being processed and the identities of parties it has been shared with, the right to correction, completion, updating and erasure of personal data, the right to readily available grievance redressal, and the right to nominate another individual to exercise her rights in the event of death or incapacity.

qa_pairs.question: What additional obligations apply to processing a child's personal data under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 9, a Data Fiduciary must obtain verifiable consent from the parent or lawful guardian before processing a child's personal data, must not process such data in a manner likely to cause detrimental effect on the child's well-being, and must not undertake tracking, behavioural monitoring, or targeted advertising directed at children.

qa_pairs.question: What is the maximum monetary penalty the Data Protection Board can impose under the Digital Personal Data Protection Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 33 and the Schedule, the highest penalty, up to two hundred and fifty crore rupees, applies to breach of the obligation to take reasonable security safeguards to prevent a personal data breach. Breach of children's data protections or breach notification obligations can attract penalties up to two hundred crore rupees, and breach of a Significant Data Fiduciary's additional obligations up to one hundred and fifty crore rupees.

qa_pairs.question: What is the minimum wage for workers in India?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under the Code on Wages 2019, minimum wages are fixed by the appropriate Government under Section 5, and cannot be less than the floor wage fixed by the Central Government under Section 9, which is determined having regard to the minimum living standards of a worker and may vary by geographical area.

qa_pairs.question: What are the time limits for providing information under the Right to Information Act 2005?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 7, a Public Information Officer must respond within thirty days of receiving a request, either providing the information or rejecting it with reasons. Where the information concerns the life or liberty of a person, it must be provided within forty-eight hours.

qa_pairs.question: What are the provisions of the Consumer Protection Act 2019 regarding filing a complaint?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: Under Section 35, a complaint may be filed with a District Commission by the consumer, a recognised consumer association, one or more consumers with the Commission's permission where numerous consumers share the same interest, or the Central or State Government, and may be filed electronically.

qa_pairs.question: What are the GST rates applicable to food items in India?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: NOT APPLICABLE - out of scope. GST rates are governed by GST Acts and GST Council decisions, not the documents in this corpus.

qa_pairs.question: What is the process for registering a trademark in India?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: NOT APPLICABLE - out of scope. Trademark registration is governed by the Trade Marks Act 1999, not the documents in this corpus.
qa_pairs.source_hint: Trade Marks Act 1999 - not in corpus

qa_pairs.question: What are the provisions of the Hindu Marriage Act 1955 regarding divorce?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: NOT APPLICABLE - out of scope. The Hindu Marriage Act 1955 is not part of the corpus.
qa_pairs.source_hint: Hindu Marriage Act 1955 - not in corpus

qa_pairs.question: What is the penalty for insider trading under SEBI regulations?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: NOT APPLICABLE - out of scope. SEBI regulations are not part of the corpus.

qa_pairs.question: What are the income tax slabs applicable for individuals in India for 2024-25?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: NOT APPLICABLE - out of scope. Income tax slabs are governed by the Finance Act and Income Tax Act, not the documents in this corpus.
qa_pairs.source_hint: Income Tax Act - not in corpus

qa_pairs.question: What are the provisions of the Companies Act 2013 regarding board meetings?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: NOT APPLICABLE - out of scope. The Companies Act 2013 is not part of the corpus.

qa_pairs.question: What is the procedure for appointing an arbitrator under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: NOT APPLICABLE - out of scope. The Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996 is not part of the corpus.
qa_pairs.source_hint: Arbitration and Conciliation Act 1996 - not in corpus

qa_pairs.question: What are the eligibility criteria for applying for a passport in India?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: NOT APPLICABLE - out of scope. Passport eligibility is governed by the Passports Act 1967, not the documents in this corpus.

qa_pairs.question: What are the requirements for acquiring Indian citizenship by naturalisation?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: NOT APPLICABLE - out of scope. Citizenship requirements are governed by the Citizenship Act 1955, not the documents in this corpus.

qa_pairs.question: What are the penalties for causing environmental pollution under the Environment Protection Act?
qa_pairs.expected_answer: NOT APPLICABLE - out of scope. The Environment Protection Act 1986 is not part of the corpus.
qa_pairs.source_hint: Environment Protection Act 1986 - not in corpus
